name: Diomedes
description: Diomedes, son of Tydeus and Deipyle, was a King of Argos and one of the greatest heroes of the Trojan War. Renowned for his courage, skill in battle, and divine favor, Diomedes was second only to Achilles in prowess among the Achaean warriors. He distinguished himself repeatedly on the battlefield, even daring to wound the gods Ares and Aphrodite when they intervened in the conflict. He was a trusted advisor and ally to other heroes such as Odysseus and Nestor. His Aristeia in the Iliad showcases his unmatched fighting ability and unwavering determination. After the Trojan War, Diomedes faced various trials and tribulations, eventually settling in Italy where he founded several cities and was worshipped as a hero. His legacy as a fearless warrior and a pious leader cemented his place among the most revered figures in Greek mythology. Diomedes's story exemplifies the ideals of heroic virtue, courage, and the importance of honoring the gods, even in the face of adversity. He is remembered as a key figure in the Trojan War and a symbol of Achaean strength.
subtitle: Argive King, Fearless Warrior of Troy
achievements: Competing in the funeral games of Patroclus
Leading the Argive contingent at Troy

keyMyths.title: Diomedes Wounds Ares
keyMyths.description: During the Trojan War, Athena grants Diomedes the ability to see the gods on the battlefield. With her guidance, he wounds Ares, the god of war, forcing him to retreat from the fighting.

keyMyths.title: Diomedes and Aphrodite
keyMyths.description: When Aphrodite attempts to rescue her son Aeneas from Diomedes, the hero wounds her as well. She flees to Olympus, complaining of her injury to Zeus.

keyMyths.title: The Palladium Theft
keyMyths.description: According to some accounts, Diomedes and Odysseus infiltrated Troy and stole the Palladium, a sacred statue of Athena believed to protect the city, thus contributing to its eventual fall.

sources.author: Homer
sources.description: The primary source for Diomedes's exploits in the Trojan War.
